title: Charmed Life - Chapter 18 (Fragment 2)
text:
“Oh, look!” said Janet. “There’s a cat.”

It was a large stripy tom. It stood arched suspiciously beside a clump of primroses, not sure whether to run away or not. It looked at Janet. It looked at Cat. And Cat knew it. Though it was firmly and definitely a cat, there was just a suggestion of a violin about the shape of its face.

He laughed. Everything made him happy in that place. “That’s old Fiddle,” he said. “He used to be my violin. What’s he doing here?”

Janet knelt down and held out her hand. “Here, Fiddle. Here, puss.” Fiddle’s nature must have been softened by being in that dell. He let Janet rub his chin and stroke him. Then, in the most unheard-of way, he let Janet pick him up and stand up hugging him. He even purred. Janet’s face glowed. She could almost have been Gwendolen coming home from a witchcraft lesson, except that she looked kinder. She winked at Cat. “I love all kinds of Cat!”

Cat laughed. He put out his left hand and stroked Fiddle’s head. It felt strange. He could feel the wood of the violin. He took his hand away quickly.

They went on through a white spread of narcissi, smelling like paradise, Janet still carrying Fiddle. There had been no white flowers until then. Cat began to be almost sure that the garden was moving around them of its own accord. When he stepped among bluebells, and then big red tulips, he was sure. He almost—but not quite—saw the trees softly and gently sliding about at the sides of what he could see. They slid him among buttercups and cow-parsley, into a sunny, sloping stretch. And here was a wild rose, tangled with a creeper covered in great blue flowers. Cat could definitely feel the sliding movement now. They were being moved around and down somehow. If he thought about the way the garden had also been moving about in the Castle grounds, he started to feel almost as sick as he did in the car. He found it was best just to keep walking and looking.

When they slid through the trees among flowers of high summer, Janet noticed too. “Aren’t we getting a lightning tour of the year?” she said. “I feel as if I’m running down a moving staircase.”

It was more than the ordinary year. Fig trees, olives, and date palms moved them around into a small desert, where there were cacti like tormented cucumbers and spiny green armchairs. Some had bright flowers on them. The sun burned down. But they had hardly time to get uncomfortable before the trees circled around them again and brought them into a richer, sadder light, and autumn flowers. They had barely got used to that when the trees put out berries, turned amber and lost their leaves. They moved towards a thick holly, full of red berries. It was colder. Fiddle did not like this part. He struggled out of Janet’s arms and ran away to warmer climes.

“Which are the gates to other worlds?” Janet said, brought back to a sense of purpose.

“Soon, I think,” said Cat. He felt them coming to the center of the garden. He had seldom felt anything magical so strongly.

The trees and bushes around them now were embalmed in frost. They could see bright berries in bright casings of ice. Yet Janet had scarcely time to rub her arms and shiver before a tree met them that was a wintry mass of pink blossom. Straight stalks of winter jasmine hung from the next, in lines of small yellow stars. And then came a mighty black thorn tree, twisted in all directions. It was just putting out a few white blossoms.

vocabulary.definition: Agreement or harmony between people or things.
vocabulary.example: The garden was moving around them of its own accord.

vocabulary.definition: A honey-yellow or golden-brown colour.
vocabulary.example: The trees put out berries, turned amber and lost their leaves.

vocabulary.definition: Curved or bent into the shape of an arch.
vocabulary.example: It stood arched suspiciously beside a clump of primroses.

vocabulary.definition: Forestal or meadow plants with drooping bell-shaped blue flowers.
vocabulary.example: He stepped among bluebells, and then big red tulips.

vocabulary.definition: Protective outer layers or coverings.
vocabulary.example: Bright berries in bright casings of ice.

vocabulary.definition: Regions or places of particular climate.
vocabulary.example: He ran away to warmer climes.

vocabulary.definition: A tall wild plant with white flowers in umbels, common in Europe.
vocabulary.example: They slid him among buttercups and cow-parsley.

vocabulary.definition: A plant that grows along the ground or up walls or trees by clinging or twining.
vocabulary.example: Tangled with a creeper covered in great blue flowers.

vocabulary.definition: Preserved or filled with a strong pleasant scent.
vocabulary.example: The trees and bushes around them now were embalmed in frost.

vocabulary.definition: A thin layer of ice that forms when water vapour freezes on a cold surface.
vocabulary.example: Embalmed in frost.

vocabulary.definition: To shine faintly with a steady light or to look radiant.
vocabulary.example: Janet’s face glowed.

vocabulary.definition: A plant with small white or yellow fragrant flowers.
vocabulary.example: Straight stalks of winter jasmine hung from the next.

vocabulary.definition: Very large or strong.
vocabulary.example: A mighty black thorn tree, twisted in all directions.

vocabulary.definition: Flowering plants with trumpet-shaped flowers, usually yellow or white.
vocabulary.example: They went on through a white spread of narcissi, smelling like paradise.

vocabulary.definition: Herbaceous plants with pale yellow flowers, typically blooming in early spring.
vocabulary.example: Beside a clump of primroses, not sure whether to run away or not.

vocabulary.definition: (of a cat) To make a soft vibrating sound expressing contentment.
vocabulary.example: He even purred.

vocabulary.definition: A feeling of determination and having a clear goal or mission.
vocabulary.example: Brought back to a sense of purpose.

vocabulary.definition: Covered with spines or thorns; prickly.
vocabulary.example: Tormented cucumbers and spiny green armchairs.

vocabulary.definition: The act of gently moving the hand over a surface, especially to show affection.
vocabulary.example: He put out his left hand and stroked Fiddle’s head.

vocabulary.definition: An idea or impression given indirectly or faintly.
vocabulary.example: There was just a suggestion of a violin about the shape of its face.

vocabulary.definition: Twisted together in a confused mass.
vocabulary.example: A wild rose, tangled with a creeper covered in great blue flowers.

vocabulary.definition: Suffering severe mental or physical pain.
vocabulary.example: Cacti like tormented cucumbers.

vocabulary.definition: Bent or turned into a different shape; contorted.
vocabulary.example: Twisted in all directions.

vocabulary.definition: Very unusual or remarkable; unprecedented.
vocabulary.example: In the most unheard-of way, he let Janet pick him up and stand up hugging him.

vocabulary.definition: Evil or morally wrong; (Note: not in the text, skip)
To close and open one eye quickly as a signal or to convey a message.
vocabulary.example: She winked at Cat.
